walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Three Taverns
walkthrough.text: Let's get Nidavellir set up. First, find the three tavern boards. They are called the Laughing Goblin, the Dancing Dragon, and the Shining Horse. Place all three side by side in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. These taverns are where you will recruit dwarves each round.

walkthrough.title: Separate the Dwarf Cards by Age
walkthrough.text: Find the dwarf cards. These are the cards with dwarves on the front in five different colors. Separate them into two decks: Age one and Age two. You can tell them apart by the number on the back. Shuffle each deck separately and set them in two face-down piles near the taverns.

walkthrough.title: Deal Dwarf Cards to the Taverns
walkthrough.text: Now deal dwarf cards from the Age one deck face up to each tavern. The number of cards per tavern depends on your player count. In a two or three player game, deal two cards per tavern. In a four or five player game, deal three cards per tavern. These are the dwarves available for recruitment this round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Hero Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the hero cards. These are the larger, more impressive-looking cards with powerful characters on them. Lay them all face up in a row near the taverns where everyone can see them. Heroes are recruited during the game when you complete a set of five different dwarf classes.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Exchange Board
walkthrough.text: Place the exchange board on the table. This is where all the upgrade coins live. Organize the coin tokens by value on the board, from the lowest values up to the highest. You will visit this board whenever you upgrade one of your coins during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Distinction Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the five distinction cards. Each one corresponds to one of the five dwarf classes. Place them face up near the taverns. These are awarded at the end of Age one to whoever has the most dwarves of each class. Each distinction grants a bonus ability or points.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Gem Track
walkthrough.text: Place the gem track board near the play area. This track determines who goes first when there is a tie during bidding. Players will place their gem tokens on this track in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Coin Purses
walkthrough.text: Give each player a coin purse. This is your personal player board with five slots for holding coins. The purse keeps your coins hidden from other players during the bidding phase. Only you can see what coins you have available.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Each player takes five starting coins with values of zero, two, three, four, and five. Place them in the five slots of your coin purse. These coins are your bidding currency for the entire game. The zero coin might seem useless now, but it will grow through upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Take Gem Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a gem token of their chosen color. Place your gem on the gem track in a random order. The track determines tiebreaker priority when two players bid the same value at a tavern.

walkthrough.title: Overview of Game Length
walkthrough.text: Nidavellir plays over eight rounds total. The first four rounds use the Age one dwarf deck. Then you switch to the Age two deck for the final four rounds. Age two dwarves are more powerful and worth more points. At the end of all eight rounds, you add up your bravery points and the highest score wins.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is it for setup. Each player should have a coin purse with five coins, a gem on the track, and no dwarves yet. The taverns should each have face-up dwarf cards, the heroes should be visible, and the exchange board should be stocked with coins. Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Nidavellir is a blind bidding and set collection game set in Norse mythology. You are an Elvaland, recruiting dwarves from taverns to build an army against the dragon Fafnir. The unique twist is that your coins grow in value during the game through upgrades, and collecting dwarves of all five classes unlocks powerful heroes.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round has three main phases. First, everyone simultaneously places bids on the three taverns. Second, you reveal bids and resolve each tavern one at a time, recruiting dwarves. Third, you upgrade your lowest coin. Then you refill the taverns and start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Place Bids
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, all players simultaneously choose three of their five coins and assign one to each tavern. Place your chosen coin face down on each tavern board so nobody can see what you bid. The two coins you did not assign stay in your purse. This is the heart of the game, because you are making blind decisions about where to bid high and where to bid low.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Reveal Bids at a Tavern
walkthrough.text: Now resolve the taverns one at a time, in order. At each tavern, all players flip their bid coins face up at the same time. The player who bid the highest value gets to pick first. They choose one of the face-up dwarf cards at that tavern and add it to their army. Then the second-highest bidder picks, and so on.

walkthrough.title: Breaking Ties with the Gem Track
walkthrough.text: When two or more players bid the same value at a tavern, the gem track breaks the tie. Whoever is higher on the gem track picks first. After using your priority, your gem moves to the bottom of the track. This matters more than you might think. Managing your position on the gem track is a subtle but important part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Resolve All Three Taverns
walkthrough.text: Repeat the reveal and draft process for each of the three taverns. You will recruit one dwarf from each tavern where you bid, so you gain up to three dwarves per round. Place each recruited dwarf face up in front of you, organized by class. Your growing dwarf army is public information.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Coin Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Here is the brilliant part. After all three taverns are resolved, look at your three bid coins. Whichever coin was your lowest bid gets upgraded. Take that coin and add its value to the value of one of the two coins that stayed in your purse. Replace the upgraded coin with a new coin from the exchange board equal to the combined value.

walkthrough.title: Coin Upgrade Example
walkthrough.text: Let's say you bid your zero, three, and five coins at the three taverns. Your zero was the lowest bid. The two coins left in your purse are your two and four. You choose the four to combine with your zero. Zero plus four equals four, so you swap your zero coin for a four coin from the exchange board. Your coin set is now two, three, four, four, five.

walkthrough.title: The Five Dwarf Classes
walkthrough.text: Every dwarf belongs to one of five classes, each with its own color and scoring method. Warriors are red and score the sum of their bravery values. Hunters are green and score based on how many you collect using a triangular number scale. Miners are blue and score only the single highest miner value. Explorers are teal and score the sum of all explorer values. Blacksmiths are purple and score on an escalating scale based on count.

walkthrough.title: Recruiting Heroes
walkthrough.text: Whenever you have at least one dwarf of each of the five classes that has not already triggered a hero, you immediately recruit a hero from the hero row. Heroes are very powerful. They count as a class of your choice and have high bravery values or special abilities. Recruiting a hero can even chain into another hero if the hero itself completes a new set of five.

walkthrough.title: The Age Transition
walkthrough.text: After four rounds of Age one, the game pauses for a special step. Award the five distinction cards. Each distinction goes to the player with the most dwarves of that class. Then remove any remaining Age one cards from the taverns and start dealing from the Age two deck. Age two dwarves have higher bravery values.

walkthrough.title: Distinction Cards
walkthrough.text: Each distinction card gives a unique bonus to whoever has the majority of that dwarf class at the end of Age one. For example, the Hunter distinction might give you extra points or an immediate ability. If players are tied for the most of a class, the player higher on the gem track wins the distinction. These bonuses can significantly impact the second half of the game.

walkthrough.title: Refilling the Taverns
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round, refill each tavern with new face-up dwarf cards from the current age deck. The number of cards dealt matches the setup count based on player count. This gives everyone fresh options to bid on next round.

walkthrough.title: Royal Treasure
walkthrough.text: Some heroes grant royal treasure tiles when recruited. These tiles provide end-game scoring bonuses based on specific conditions. Keep them face up near your dwarf army so you remember to score them at the end.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after eight rounds. Add up your bravery points from all sources. Score each dwarf class using its unique method. Add bravery values from all your hero cards. Add any distinction card bonuses. Add any royal treasure points. Finally, add the value of your single highest coin to your score.

walkthrough.title: Determining the Winner
walkthrough.text: The player with the highest total bravery points wins the game. If there is a tie, the player with the highest single coin value wins. Now that you know the rules, let's try a practice round to see everything in action.

bullets: Blind bidding + set collection in Norse mythology

Highest bravery points after 8 rounds wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Round
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Nidavellir! We will walk through a scripted first round with three example players so you can see how bidding, recruiting, and coin upgrades work. Follow along even if you have a different player count. Let's get started.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with five coins worth zero, two, three, four, and five. The three taverns each have dwarf cards dealt face up. Remember, you need to assign three coins to three taverns and keep two in your purse. Your purse coins matter because one of them will be used to upgrade your lowest bid coin.

walkthrough.title: Player One Plans Their Bids
walkthrough.text: Player one looks at the taverns. The Laughing Goblin has a great hunter card. The Dancing Dragon has a decent warrior. The Shining Horse has a miner. Player one really wants that hunter, so they place their five coin face down on the Laughing Goblin. They put the three on the Dancing Dragon and the zero on the Shining Horse. The two and four stay in the purse.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Plans Their Bids
walkthrough.text: Player two also wants the hunter at the Laughing Goblin. They bid their four coin there, put the five on the Dancing Dragon for the warrior, and the two on the Shining Horse. Their zero and three stay in the purse. Both players want the same dwarf, but only one can get first pick.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Plans Their Bids
walkthrough.text: Player three decides to focus on a different strategy. They want the miner at the Shining Horse, so they bid their five there. They put the three on the Laughing Goblin and the two on the Dancing Dragon. The zero and four stay in the purse.

walkthrough.title: Reveal at the Laughing Goblin
walkthrough.text: Time to resolve the first tavern. Everyone flips their Laughing Goblin coin. Player one bid five, player two bid four, player three bid three. Player one wins with the highest bid and takes the hunter card they wanted. Player two picks next, and player three gets the remaining card.

walkthrough.title: Reveal at the Dancing Dragon
walkthrough.text: Now the Dancing Dragon. Player two bid five, player one bid three, player three bid two. Player two wins easily and takes the warrior they wanted. The remaining players pick from what is left.

walkthrough.title: Reveal at the Shining Horse
walkthrough.text: Finally the Shining Horse. Player three bid five, player one bid zero, player two bid two. Player three gets first pick and grabs the miner they targeted. Notice how player one intentionally bid zero here because they did not care about this tavern.

walkthrough.title: Why Bidding Zero Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Player one bid their zero coin on the tavern they cared about least. This was actually a great move. The zero coin will now be their lowest bid, which means it gets upgraded. The lower the coin you upgrade, the more room it has to grow. Bidding zero intentionally is one of the fundamental strategies in Nidavellir.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Coin Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Player one's lowest bid was the zero coin. Their two purse coins are the two and the four. They choose to combine the zero with the four. Zero plus four equals four, so they swap the zero coin for a four coin from the exchange board. Their coins are now two, three, four, four, five. That useless zero coin just became a real asset.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Coin Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Player two's lowest bid was the two coin. Their purse coins are the zero and three. They combine the two with the three. Two plus three equals five, so they swap their two coin for a five coin. But wait — player two left their zero coin in the purse. That zero coin is stuck and will not get upgraded this round because it was not the lowest bid. This is a common beginner mistake.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Leaving the Zero in Your Purse
walkthrough.text: Player two made a critical error. By leaving the zero coin in the purse instead of bidding it, they missed the chance to upgrade it. The zero coin should almost always be one of your three bids, not kept in the purse. When it is bid, it will be your lowest coin and get upgraded. When it sits in the purse, it stays at zero and drags you down.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Coin Upgrade
walkthrough.text: Player three's lowest bid was the two coin. Their purse coins are the zero and four. They combine the two with the four. Two plus four equals six, so they swap their two coin for a six coin. Player three now has the highest single coin in the game so far.

walkthrough.title: Check Your Dwarf Collection
walkthrough.text: After round one, each player has three dwarf cards. Look at the classes you collected. Do you have different classes, or did you double up? Remember, you need one of each of the five classes to recruit a hero. Start thinking about which classes you are missing and plan your bids for next round accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Class Diversity
walkthrough.text: Let's say player two collected two warriors and a hunter. They got excited about high-value warriors and ignored diversity. But heroes are worth massive points, and you need all five classes to recruit one. Chasing duplicates of one class early means you fall behind on heroes while your opponents complete sets first.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Overbidding Every Tavern
walkthrough.text: New players often try to win every tavern by bidding their highest coins everywhere. But this is counterproductive. If you bid high at all three taverns, your lowest bid is still relatively high, which means your coin upgrade is less impactful. The sweet spot is bidding one high coin where it matters most and one low coin where you can afford to lose.

walkthrough.title: Refill and Prepare for Round Two
walkthrough.text: Now deal new dwarf cards to each tavern from the Age one deck. Before you bid, scan all three taverns and decide which dwarves you need most. Check your opponents' collections too. If someone is close to completing a set of five classes, they might bid aggressively to finish it. Anticipating their bids helps you choose where to compete and where to concede.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Forgetting About Distinctions
walkthrough.text: Some new players forget that distinction cards are awarded at the end of Age one. If you have been ignoring a class entirely, you have no shot at that distinction. On the other hand, investing a little in a class where nobody else is competing can earn you a free distinction bonus with minimal effort. Keep an eye on the class counts across all players.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues for seven more rounds. Each round follows the same pattern: bid, reveal, recruit, upgrade. As your coins grow in value, the bidding wars get more intense. After round four, you will award distinctions and switch to the Age two deck. Keep building your dwarf army, chase those hero unlocks, and grow your coins as high as possible.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have just played through your first round of Nidavellir. You learned how to bid coins at taverns, how the upgrade mechanism works, and you saw three common beginner mistakes: leaving the zero coin in your purse, ignoring class diversity, and overbidding every tavern. You are ready to play the full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common traps to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all five dwarf classes and key game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for classes and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's cover the biggest mistakes new players make. First, leaving the zero coin in your purse instead of bidding it. That zero needs to be bid so it gets upgraded. Second, chasing duplicates of one class while ignoring others, which delays hero recruitment. Third, overbidding at every tavern so your lowest bid is too high for a meaningful upgrade. Fourth, forgetting about distinctions at the end of Age one. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the worst beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Always Bid Your Zero Coin
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important rule for new players. Your zero coin should be bid at a tavern every single round until it has been upgraded. The coin upgrade targets your lowest bid, so your zero will always be the one that gets upgraded. Pair it with the highest coin in your purse to maximize the upgrade. A zero combined with a five becomes a five coin in one round. If you leave the zero in your purse, it just sits there being worthless.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Class Diversity Early
walkthrough.text: Heroes are some of the highest-scoring cards in the game, and you need one dwarf of each of the five classes to recruit one. In the first few rounds, focus on collecting different classes rather than stacking one class. Getting your first hero by round three or four gives you a massive advantage because heroes count as a class of your choice, which makes completing your next set even faster.

walkthrough.title: Balance Winning and Upgrading
walkthrough.text: Every round presents a tension between winning dwarves and upgrading coins. If you bid all your high coins, you win great dwarves but your upgrade is weak because your lowest bid is still relatively high. If you bid all low coins, your upgrade is great but you get last pick at every tavern. The sweet spot is one strong bid where it matters most, one medium bid you are fine losing, and one low bid you intentionally sacrifice for the upgrade.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Since everyone's dwarf army is public information, pay attention to what your opponents are collecting. If someone is one class away from completing a hero set, they will likely bid high for that class. Use this knowledge to either compete strategically or avoid expensive bidding wars you cannot win. Also watch their coin values. After a few rounds, you can estimate their coin range and predict whether they will bid high or low at each tavern.

walkthrough.title: Think About Distinctions Early
walkthrough.text: Distinction cards are awarded at the end of Age one, and they provide powerful bonuses for the second half of the game. Glance at what classes your opponents are collecting. If nobody is investing heavily in explorers, even two or three explorers might be enough to win that distinction. The easiest distinctions to win are the ones nobody else is fighting over.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Scoring Methods
walkthrough.text: Each dwarf class scores differently, and understanding these differences is crucial. Hunters and blacksmiths scale exponentially with count, so stacking them is rewarding. Miners only score the single highest value, so one great miner beats three mediocre ones. Warriors and explorers score by summing values, so quantity and quality both matter. Plan your collection strategy around these scoring methods rather than grabbing dwarves at random.

walkthrough.title: Your Highest Coin Is Points
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, your single highest coin value is added directly to your score. A coin worth twenty or more is like having an extra high-value dwarf card. This means coin upgrades are not just about winning bids. They are a scoring engine in their own right. In the late game, consider whether upgrading a coin from eighteen to twenty-three might be worth more than winning a particular dwarf card.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't leave the 0-coin in your purse — bid it every round
Don't chase duplicates of one class — diversify for heroes
Don't overbid everywhere — sacrifice one tavern for a strong upgrade
Don't forget distinctions at end of Age 1

summary.title: Always Bid Your Zero Coin
bullets: 0-coin should be bid (not kept in purse) every round until upgraded
Lowest bid gets upgraded → 0 is always the lowest
Pair with highest purse coin: 0 + 5 = instant 5-coin
Leaving 0 in purse = wasted upgrade opportunity

summary.title: Prioritize Class Diversity Early
bullets: Need 1 of each of 5 classes to recruit a hero
Heroes are massive point sources — prioritize set completion
First hero by round 3-4 gives huge advantage
Heroes count as any class → easier to chain into more heroes

summary.title: Balance Winning and Upgrading
bullets: Tension: winning dwarves vs. growing coins
Sweet spot: 1 high bid, 1 medium bid, 1 low bid
Low bid = strong upgrade, high bid = first pick
All-high bids → weak upgrade. All-low bids → last pick everywhere

summary.title: Read Your Opponents
bullets: Dwarf armies are public — track what classes opponents need
Predict aggressive bids when opponents are 1 class from a hero
Avoid bidding wars you can't win
Estimate opponents' coin ranges after a few rounds

summary.title: Think About Distinctions Early
bullets: Distinctions awarded at end of Age 1 for class majorities
Easiest to win in classes nobody else is pursuing
Even 2-3 dwarves can win an uncontested distinction
Distinction bonuses impact the entire second half of the game

summary.title: Understand the Scoring Methods
bullets: Hunters/Blacksmiths: exponential scaling by count — stack them
Miners: only highest value counts — 1 great miner > 3 mediocre
Warriors/Explorers: sum of values — quantity + quality both matter
Plan collection strategy around scoring methods

summary.title: Your Highest Coin Is Points
bullets: Highest coin value added directly to final score
A 20+ coin = as many points as a strong dwarf card
Coin upgrades are a scoring engine, not just bidding power
Late game: consider upgrade value vs. dwarf card value

walkthrough.title: The Coin Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Nidavellir is to prioritize coin growth above all else in the early game. The idea is to intentionally lose taverns by bidding low, which gives you massive upgrades round after round. By mid-game, your coins are so high that you can outbid everyone when it actually matters. A player with coins worth ten, twelve, and fifteen has first pick at every tavern while still upgrading aggressively.

walkthrough.title: The Hero Chain
walkthrough.text: This is the most exciting play in Nidavellir. When you recruit a hero, that hero counts as a class of your choice. If you choose a class that completes another set of five, you immediately recruit a second hero. In theory, you can chain multiple heroes in a single round. To set this up, collect four of the five classes in two separate sets, then recruit one dwarf that completes both sets in sequence. Hero chains can swing thirty or more points in a single turn.

walkthrough.title: Hunter and Blacksmith Stacking
walkthrough.text: Hunters and blacksmiths score on exponential scales based on count. Six hunters score twenty-one points. Seven score twenty-eight. These numbers quickly outpace every other scoring method. If you commit to stacking one of these classes, you can build an enormous point engine. The key is claiming the corresponding distinction at the end of Age one, which usually gives you a bonus that amplifies this strategy further.

walkthrough.title: The Miner Snipe
walkthrough.text: Miners are unique because only your single highest miner scores. This means you can ignore miners entirely and then snipe one high-value miner in the late game. While your opponents invest multiple picks into miners, you spend those picks on classes that reward quantity. Then in Age two, when a high-value miner appears, you make one strong bid and score nearly as many miner points as someone who collected four mediocre ones.

walkthrough.title: Distinction Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Distinctions are awarded to the player with the most dwarves of each class at the end of Age one. Advanced players manipulate this by tracking opponents' counts and making targeted picks in rounds three and four. If you are tied with an opponent for a class, remember that the gem track breaks ties. You can time your bids to either secure gem track priority or to push past the tie with one final recruit.

walkthrough.title: Gem Track Management
walkthrough.text: The gem track is easy to overlook, but skilled players use it deliberately. Being high on the gem track means you win ties, which is especially valuable when coins are clustered around similar values. You can manipulate your position by intentionally tying at taverns where your gem priority matters less, dropping to the bottom, and then climbing back when opponents use their priority. In tight games, gem track position can determine who gets a crucial dwarf.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Tavern Spread
walkthrough.text: Before placing bids, evaluate all three taverns as a group. Ask yourself which tavern has the most competitive card, which one has a card that fits your collection, and which one is least important. Then assign your highest coin to the competitive tavern, your strategic pick to the medium tavern, and your sacrifice coin to the one you care least about. Experienced players also consider what their opponents need and predict where the bidding wars will be.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Coin Scoring
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, your highest coin value is added to your score as raw points. A coin worth twenty-five is worth as much as a very strong dwarf card. This means that even if you are behind on dwarves, a massive coin can close the gap. In the last two rounds, consider whether growing your coin further might score more points than fighting for a particular dwarf. The best players balance both.

summary.title: The Coin Engine Strategy
bullets: Prioritize coin upgrades early — intentionally lose taverns
Massive coins by mid-game = first pick when it matters
Player with 10, 12, 15 coins dominates late-game bidding
Sacrifice early dwarves for long-term coin dominance

summary.title: The Hero Chain
bullets: Hero counts as any class → can complete another set instantly
Setup: 4 of 5 classes in two separate sets, then trigger both
Hero chains can swing 30+ points in a single turn
One of the most powerful plays in the game

summary.title: Hunter and Blacksmith Stacking
bullets: Exponential scaling: 6 hunters = 21 pts, 7 = 28 pts
Quickly outpaces other scoring methods at high counts
Claim the matching distinction at end of Age 1 to amplify
Commit early — half-measures don't work with exponential scoring

summary.title: The Miner Snipe
bullets: Only highest single miner scores — ignore miners early
Save picks for classes that reward quantity
Snipe 1 high-value miner in Age 2 for near-max miner points
Opponents investing 4 picks in mediocre miners gain little advantage

summary.title: Distinction Manipulation
bullets: Track opponents' class counts heading into rounds 3-4
Tied for a class? Gem track breaks the tie
One targeted pick in round 4 can steal a distinction
Distinction bonuses compound throughout Age 2

summary.title: Gem Track Management
bullets: High gem position = win ties at taverns
Intentionally tie at low-priority taverns to manipulate position
Climb back when opponents use their priority
In tight games, gem track decides crucial picks

summary.title: Reading the Tavern Spread
bullets: Evaluate all 3 taverns as a group before bidding
Highest coin → most competitive tavern
Lowest coin → least important tavern (sacrifice for upgrade)
Predict opponents' priorities based on their collections

summary.title: Late Game Coin Scoring
bullets: Highest coin value = raw end-game points
A 25-coin is worth as much as a top-tier dwarf card
Last 2 rounds: weigh coin growth vs. dwarf recruitment
Best players maximize both coins and dwarves

entries.definition: One of the five dwarf classes. Warriors score by summing the bravery values printed on all warrior cards in your army. More warriors with higher values means more points. Straightforward and easy to evaluate.

One of the five dwarf classes. Hunters score based on how many you collect using a triangular number scale: one hunter scores one point, two score three, three score six, four score ten, five score fifteen, six score twenty-one, and so on. Scales exponentially with count.

One of the five dwarf classes. Miners score only the single highest bravery value among all your miner cards. Collecting multiple miners does not increase your score unless a new one has a higher value. One great miner is better than several mediocre ones.
One of the five dwarf classes. Explorers score by summing all explorer bravery values. Similar to warriors but typically with different value distributions. Both quantity and quality matter.

One of the five dwarf classes. Blacksmiths score on an escalating scale based on how many you collect, similar to hunters but with different point values. Stacking blacksmiths can yield very high scores at large counts.

Powerful unique characters recruited when you have at least one dwarf of each of the five classes that has not yet triggered a hero. Heroes count as a class of your choice and have high bravery values or special abilities. Can chain into additional heroes if they complete another set.

When a recruited hero's chosen class completes another set of five different classes, you immediately recruit an additional hero. Multiple heroes can be chained in a single trigger. Requires careful setup across two or more incomplete sets.

Five special cards, one per dwarf class, awarded at the end of Age one to the player with the most dwarves of that class. Ties broken by gem track position. Each provides a unique bonus ability or points for the rest of the game.
Tiles granted by certain hero cards when recruited. They provide end-game scoring bonuses based on specific conditions in your army or coin collection. Keep them visible as a reminder to score them.

Your personal player board with five slots for coins. Keeps your coins hidden from other players. You assign three coins to taverns and keep two in the purse each round. The purse coins are used during the upgrade phase.

Every player begins with five coins valued at zero, two, three, four, and five. These are placed in the coin purse at the start of the game. Through upgrades, these coins grow in value throughout the game.

After resolving all three taverns, your lowest bid coin is upgraded. Its value is added to one of the two coins remaining in your purse, and the upgraded coin is replaced with a new coin of the combined value from the exchange board.
The shared board holding all available coin tokens organized by value. When you upgrade a coin, take the new value coin from this board. When returning an old coin, place it back on the board.

The core mechanism of Nidavellir. All players simultaneously place coins face down on taverns without knowing what others bid. Bids are revealed simultaneously at each tavern. Creates tension and rewards reading opponents.

At game end, the value of your single highest coin is added directly to your bravery point total. A coin worth twenty or more is equivalent to a strong dwarf card in points. Coin growth is both a bidding tool and a scoring engine.
One of the three tavern boards where dwarves are recruited. Taverns are resolved in a fixed order each round. Players bid one coin face down at each tavern.
The second of the three tavern boards. Like all taverns, it receives fresh dwarf cards each round and resolves bids in order of highest to lowest coin value.
The third of the three tavern boards. The last tavern resolved each round. Strategic players sometimes sacrifice this tavern with a low bid to fuel their coin upgrade.
A colored token representing each player on the gem track. Used to break ties when two players bid the same value at a tavern. After using priority, the gem drops to the bottom of the track.

A track that determines tiebreaker priority when players bid equal values. The player whose gem is higher on the track picks first. After using priority, their gem moves to the bottom. Managing position is a subtle strategic element.

The first half of the game, consisting of rounds one through four. Uses the Age one dwarf deck with lower bravery values. At the end of Age one, distinction cards are awarded before switching to the Age two deck.

The second half of the game, consisting of rounds five through eight. Uses the Age two dwarf deck with higher bravery values. Stronger dwarves and higher-value coins make the late game more competitive.

The pause between Age one and Age two. Distinction cards are awarded to class majority holders. Remaining Age one cards are removed from taverns. The Age two deck begins being dealt. A pivotal moment that can reshape the game.

The victory point currency of Nidavellir. Earned from dwarf class scoring, hero cards, distinction bonuses, royal treasure, and your highest coin value. The player with the most bravery points at the end of eight rounds wins.

The scoring method used by hunters: one card scores one point, two cards score three, three cards score six, four score ten, five score fifteen, six score twenty-one, and so on. Each additional card adds an increasing number of points.
The scoring method used by blacksmiths, similar to the triangular scale but with different point values. Like hunters, the more blacksmiths you collect, the higher the return on each additional card.
The scoring method used by warriors and explorers. Simply add up the bravery values printed on all cards of that class. Both the number of cards and the individual values matter.
The scoring method used by miners. Only the single highest bravery value among all your miner cards counts. Additional miners with lower values do not add to your score.

One dwarf of each of the five classes (warrior, hunter, miner, explorer, blacksmith) that has not yet triggered a hero recruitment. Completing a set immediately lets you recruit a hero card. Multiple sets can be completed during the game.
When two players have the same total bravery points at game end, the player with the highest single coin value wins. Coin value serves as both an in-game scoring element and the ultimate tiebreaker.
